event: Jezebel and Ahab Confronted by Elijah after Naboth's Vineyard
symbols: Elijah's red cloak — the mantle of prophetic authority, the fire of divine judgment, the blood of innocent Naboth crying for justice
Jezebel's crown — royal power corrupted, foreign idolatry enthroned in Israel, earthly authority's pretension before God's prophet
Ahab's turbaned, crouching posture — the king diminished before the prophet, royal power exposed as moral weakness
Jezebel's defiant sidelong gaze — unrepentant wickedness, the hardened heart that refuses the prophetic word
stone archway — the threshold of Naboth's vineyard, the stolen property that becomes the site of divine confrontation
Elijah's bare feet — the prophet's poverty contrasted with royal luxury, the humility of God's messenger
pointing gesture of Elijah — the prophetic accusation, the finger of God declaring judgment through his servant
description: Three figures stand in an architectural setting with stone arches. On the right, the prophet Elijah, a tall bearded barefoot figure wearing a dark robe with a dramatic red-orange cloak, raises his right hand in accusation. On the left, Jezebel and Ahab move together: Jezebel wears a golden crown over dark hair and a dark blue-green mantle, looking sideways toward Elijah with cold defiance. Ahab is partially hidden behind her, hunched and turbaned in white with a red sash, his posture conveying guilt. A tree with dark foliage frames the upper left. Blue sky shows through the stone arch.
theological_description: This painting captures one of the most dramatically charged confrontations in the Old Testament: the moment when Elijah meets King Ahab and Queen Jezebel, most likely in or near Naboth's vineyard after its rightful owner has been judicially murdered at Jezebel's instigation (1 Kings 21). Elijah stands alone on the right, but he towers over the royal pair -- a visual enactment of the biblical principle that prophetic authority, derived from God, outweighs royal power derived from human politics. Ahab crouches behind Jezebel, exactly as the biblical narrative presents him: a king who 'sold himself to do evil' because 'Jezebel his wife stirred him up' (1 Kings 21:25). Jezebel, by contrast, shows no contrition; her sidelong glare is the look of one who has killed prophets and will soon threaten to kill this one (1 Kings 19:2). Elijah's accusation -- 'Have you killed and also taken possession?' (1 Kings 21:19) -- is God's own voice pronouncing judgment on the abuse of royal power. The setting evokes Naboth's vineyard, which carries profound covenantal significance: the land belongs to the LORD and is allotted to families as a sacred trust (Leviticus 25:23). Ahab's theft is not merely a property crime but a violation of the covenant structure of Israelite society. Jesus himself would later employ the vineyard as his primary parable of Israel's unfaithful stewardship (Matthew 21:33-41). Jezebel's defiance foreshadows her grisly end (2 Kings 9:30-37), while Ahab's partial repentance (1 Kings 21:27-29) is visible in his already-broken posture.

scripture_refs.reason: God sends Elijah to confront Ahab in Naboth's vineyard and pronounce judgment on him and Jezebel
The preceding narrative of Jezebel's plot to murder Naboth and seize his vineyard for Ahab
The summary judgment that Ahab sold himself to do evil, urged on by Jezebel his wife
Ahab's partial repentance after Elijah's confrontation, visible in his crouching posture
Jezebel's death threat against Elijah after the contest on Carmel -- her defiant gaze foreshadows this hostility
The fulfillment of Elijah's prophecy: Jezebel's violent death in the plot of ground at Jezreel
The land belongs to God and cannot be permanently sold -- the covenantal principle Ahab violated
Jesus's parable of the wicked tenants, extending the vineyard-and-murder motif from Naboth to the cross
The vineyard of the LORD is the house of Israel -- the vineyard as symbol of God's covenant people
The rich who have condemned and murdered the righteous -- the prophetic tradition from Elijah to the New Testament

_meta.title: Jezebel and Ahab Met by Elijah
